front: Event: fascist Nationalists led by Francisco Franco fought the Republicans (Loyalists) of the leftist Second Spanish Republic in this 1936-1939 civil war, which the Nationalists won
Event: the poet Federico Garcia Lorca was assassinated by Franco's supporters in 1936 during this war
Event: this conflict is the setting of Ernest Hemingway's For Whom the Bell Tolls and George Orwell's Homage to Catalonia
Event: the CEDA's fall from power prompted a coup led by General Jose Sanjurjo, one of the causes of this war
Event: this war was presaged by a miners' strike that was supported in parliament by the feminist Margarita Nelken
Event: just before this war's outbreak, outrage followed the summary execution of anyone who owned a gun in the town of Casas Viejas
Figure: coined the term 'fifth column' to describe secret Nationalist sympathizers within Republican-held cities, notably during the siege of Madrid
work: The Nationalist Faction and the July 1936 Coup

front: Figure: died in a 1936 plane crash reportedly caused by excess luggage he insisted on bringing back to Spain
work: The Nationalist Faction and the July 1936 Coup

front: Figure: died in a plane crash the following year (1937), leaving Francisco Franco as the Nationalists' sole leader
work: The Nationalist Faction and the July 1936 Coup

front: Figure: nicknamed the 'Lion of the Rif' for earlier defeating Abd el-Krim's guerrilla resistance in Morocco
work: The Nationalist Faction and the July 1936 Coup

front: Ruler: led the victorious Falange to become Generalissimo, ruling Spain as dictator for 36 years until his 1975 death; merged the Falange with the Carlist movement into a single party
work: The Nationalist Faction and the July 1936 Coup

front: Event: the Trotskyist POUM militia and the anarchist CNT clashed with the government's Assault Guard in Barcelona's 1937 May Events
work: The Republican Faction and Factional Strife

front: Figure: founded and led a namesake anarchist military column of roughly 3,000 volunteers, which included the philosopher Simone Weil, that operated without direct command
work: The Republican Faction and Factional Strife

front: Institution: this Durruti-led anarchist militia fought to retake Zaragoza from General Emilio Mola
work: The Republican Faction and Factional Strife

front: Event: the Nationalists received supplies from Nazi Germany through the dummy companies HISMA and ROWAK
work: Foreign Intervention and the International Brigades

front: Institution: international volunteer units fighting for the Republicans included ones named for James Connolly and Abraham Lincoln, the Canadian Mackenzie-Papineau Battalion, and the Italian Garibaldi Brigade
work: Foreign Intervention and the International Brigades

front: Institution: these Irish Republican volunteers, subject of a Christy Moore song inspired by a 1979 book by Michael O'Riordan, fought for the Republicans
work: Foreign Intervention and the International Brigades

front: Institution: Eoin O'Duffy's fascist Blueshirts merged with the Greenshirts to form this Irish volunteer unit, which fought for Franco
work: Foreign Intervention and the International Brigades

front: Event: piracy against merchant shipping during this war was addressed at the Nyon Conference
work: Foreign Intervention and the International Brigades

front: Event: this war caused the cancellation of the alternative 'People's Olympiad' planned in Barcelona
work: Foreign Intervention and the International Brigades

front: Event: France's leftist Popular Front government, headed by Leon Blum, fractured over whether to intervene in this war
work: Foreign Intervention and the International Brigades

front: Event: the defenders' rallying cry '¡No pasaran!' ('They shall not pass!') during this siege was popularized by a speech from Dolores Ibarruri, nicknamed 'La Pasionaria' ('Passionflower')
Battle: Republican forces briefly captured this Nationalist provincial capital before Antonio Aranda led a successful counterattack

Figure: following the loss of Teruel, this prime minister ordered an unsuccessful attack on a river bend, and the Republican side ultimately surrendered after his government failed to recapture the city
Battle: Republican forces were ultimately crushed at this battle along a river
Event: this campaign helped cut off and economically strangle the Republicans, contributing to the Nationalist victory in the War in the North
Event: the German Condor Legion, codenamed Operation Rugen, bombed this sacred Basque town in 1937, destroying its 600-year-old sacred oak tree
Figure: this journalist's reporting for The Times on the bombing of Guernica helped foster international outrage
Event: the Republican government shipped roughly 510 tons of gold, nearly three-fourths of the country's reserves, to Moscow soon after this war's outbreak, in exchange for Soviet arms
Figure: this NKVD agent transported Spain's gold reserves to Moscow soon after the war's outbreak
Figure: this Republican president ordered Spain's gold reserves sent to the Soviet Union before his government was driven from Madrid and then Barcelona

Figure: as Minister of Finance, this man persuaded Prime Minister Francisco Largo Caballero to carry out the transfer of Spain's gold reserves to Moscow, for which Franco's supporters later villainized him; he subsequently became prime minister himself
Event: this affair's gold shipment culminated when four ships, loaded under the supervision of Francisco Mendez Aspe, landed in Odessa
Event: this monument near Madrid was built to honor the Spanish Civil War's dead
Event: in the two decades after this war, the development of Basque-language literature almost entirely ceased
Novel: American dynamiter Robert Jordan joins a Republican guerrilla band led by Anselmo, Pilar, and her husband Pablo to blow up a bridge in this novel
back: For Whom the Bell Tolls (Ernest Hemingway)

front: Novel: Pilar reads the protagonist's palms, and he falls in love with Maria, a former rape victim, in this novel
back: For Whom the Bell Tolls (Ernest Hemingway)

front: Memoir: this author's memoir describes how, while fighting with the POUM militia, he was shot through the neck by a sniper and failed to free his friend Georges Kopp from prison before escaping Spain
Memoir: this book's author, whom publisher Victor Gollancz (who had earlier commissioned the author's The Road to Wigan Pier) refused to publish, derided Gollancz as 'part of the Communism-racket'
Story: a Belgian priest tends to prisoners awaiting execution in a frigid hospital cellar in this short story
Story: this short story's protagonist Pablo Ibbieta unwittingly reveals the hiding place of his ally Ramon Gris, who is found hiding in a cemetery and shot by Nationalists
Novel: this novel's protagonist murders El Estirao, the lover of his sister Rosario and his wife Lola, and is written as two 'found' prison letters
back: The Family of Pascual Duarte (Camilo Jose Cela)

front: Novel: Camilo Jose Cela set this novel at the end of the Spanish Civil War
Novel: an orphan named Andrea moves into her grandmother's dilapidated home and befriends Ena at university in this existentialist novel set in the war's aftermath
Novel: Natalia struggles in relationships with Quimet and Antoni in this novel
back: The Time of the Doves (Merce Rodoreda)

front: Movement: this generation of Spanish writers was primarily active during the Spanish Civil War
Novel: the title teacher encourages her student Joyce Emily to fight and die in the Spanish Civil War in this novel
back: The Prime of Miss Jean Brodie (Muriel Spark)

front: Novel: a seamstress stitches a secret code into her dresses to aid one side in the Spanish Civil War in this novel
Novel: this novel is set in the aftermath of the Spanish Civil War, during which George Orwell served
back: The Shadow of the Wind (Carlos Ruiz Zafon)

front: Novel: this novel chronicles Rubashov's confinement, with Joseph Stalin implicitly represented as 'Number One'; its author drew on his own imprisonment during the Spanish Civil War, described in his memoir Spanish Testament
Story: this story collection includes a story describing Verschoyle's actions during the Spanish Civil War
back: A Tomb for Boris Davidovich (Danilo Kis)

front: Play: this Enrique Jardiel Poncela comedy premiered during the Spanish Civil War
back: Four Hearts with Brake and Reverse (Enrique Jardiel Poncela)

front: Play: Helene Weigel played the title character in this Bertolt Brecht play, titled for her 'rifles,' set during the Spanish Civil War
Playwright: wrote the play This Trampled Earth about the Spanish Civil War after participating in it himself
Historian: controversially labeled the Spanish Civil War a 'holocaust' in a book about it
Poem: this Pablo Neruda poem describes the Spanish Civil War's toll on Spain, ending with the refrain 'Come and see the blood in the streets!'
Poem: this Cesar Vallejo poem addresses 'children, sons of fighters' and warns the Spanish Civil War will bring an 'age of concave temples'
back: "Spain, Take This Cup From Me" (Cesar Vallejo)

front: Poem: this W.H. Auden poem repeats the refrain 'to-day the struggle' and ends 'The stars are dead. The animals will not look'
Poet: serving with the Red Cross during the Spanish Civil War, contributed poems including 'Journey to Barcelona' to a war anthology edited by Stephen Spender
Work: this Stephen Spender autobiography describes him chasing after an ex-lover who went to fight in the Spanish Civil War
Painting: this Pablo Picasso painting, a black-and-white mural-sized response to the bombing of Guernica, depicts a stabbed horse screaming in pain and a mother holding her dead child beneath a bull
Painting: a flower grows from a disembodied arm gripping a broken dagger in this painting
Work: in this etching series, which Picasso claimed predicted the Spanish Civil War, a shriveled head emerges from a knife-shaped leg grasped by an arm that itself emerges from another limb
back: The Dream and Lie of Franco (Pablo Picasso)

front: Work: Joan Miro was commissioned to paint this now-lost mural depicting a sickle-wielding Catalan peasant shortly after the Spanish Civil War's outbreak
Painting: this Salvador Dali painting, subtitled Premonition of Civil War, was inspired by the Spanish Civil War
back: Soft Construction with Boiled Beans (Salvador Dali)

front: Work: Robert Motherwell painted this series of abstract expressionist works, using varied arrangements of black and white ovals and squares, to memorialize the Spanish Civil War
back: Elegy to the Spanish Republic series (Robert Motherwell)

front: Sculpture: this Alexander Calder fountain was inspired by the Spanish Civil War

Artist: used eggs to depict eggs in her tempera paintings The Giantess and The Kitchen Garden on the Eyot, and wrote that the egg was 'the dividing line between the Big and the Small' during a wartime stay at a sanatorium
Photograph: this Robert Capa photograph depicts a Loyalist soldier recoiling as he appears to be shot in the head, his rifle slipping from his hand; Capa may have staged it
Photographer: this German photojournalist, who died during a battle in Spain, made her name covering the Spanish Civil War's frontlines
Photograph: this Gerda Taro photograph depicts a woman in heels kneeling on a beach and aiming a pistol
back: Woman Training for a Republican Militia (Gerda Taro)

front: Event: nearly 4,000 smuggled photographs from the Spanish Civil War, including work by Robert Capa, Gerda Taro, and Polish photojournalist David 'Chim' Seymour, were discovered in 2010 in three cardboard boxes in a Mexico City closet
Photograph: this Henri Cartier-Bresson photograph also depicted the Spanish Civil War's toll
back: Children Playing in the Ruins (Henri Cartier-Bresson)
